current_fact.fact_text: On May 19, 1996, Space Shuttle Endeavour lifted off on STS-77, a mission packed with experimental payloads including inflatable structures and free-flying platforms. It was a laboratory in the sky, testing futuristic technologies in the frictionless classroom of orbit.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

current_fact.match_reason: STS-77 was essentially a flying classroom, full of experimental hardware and trial-and-error in microgravity, resonating with an episode about the joy, frustration, and persistence of learning through experiments.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1971, the Soviet Union launched Mars 2, an ambitious mission carrying an orbiter and a lander toward the Red Planet. Although the lander crash‑landed, the orbiter sent back valuable data, turning a partial failure into a scientific foothold on Mars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A mission to another world named Mars 2 evokes the image of distant, slow-turning planets, in tune with this episode’s gentle, downtempo reflection on moving through life at a slower orbital speed.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1845, Sir John Franklin’s Arctic expedition departed Greenhithe, England, aiming to chart the elusive Northwest Passage. The voyage vanished into ice and legend, becoming one of exploration’s most haunting mysteries and a stark reminder of nature’s unforgiving power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both the Franklin expedition and this episode delve into the high latitudes, where human obsession meets the stark beauty and danger of Arctic waters and icebound journeys.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1962, Marilyn Monroe stepped onto the Madison Square Garden stage and delivered her breathy, almost whispered rendition of “Happy Birthday” to President John F. Kennedy. The performance was so intimate and theatrical that it turned a simple birthday song into one of the most mythologized moments in pop culture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #09 - Birthday Cake
episode.description: Today's breakfast comes with a sprinkle of stardust and a slice of birthday cake as we celebrate a pure and unique soul, Yasmin. On Sedna, we may not do years, but we sure know how to party!

queue.match_reason: A legendary birthday serenade to a president fits perfectly with an episode built around birthday celebration, stardust, and the joyfully theatrical side of marking another orbit.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1997, Mexico’s Sierra Gorda region was officially declared a biosphere reserve, protecting one of the country’s most ecologically diverse landscapes. The designation emerged from decades of grassroots activism, proving that local communities can safeguard entire ecosystems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: The protection of lush, living landscapes through community effort mirrors this episode’s slow, sunlit immersion in nature, family, and the rhythms that make an ‘endless summer’ of the Earth feel tangible.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1535, Jacques Cartier set sail on his second voyage to North America, bringing three ships, 110 men, and two Iroquois brothers he had previously taken to France. The journey mixed exploration with abduction, leaving a complicated legacy of first contact and cultural collision along the St. Lawrence River.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: Both stories unfold on ships at sea, where crews from different cultures share cramped decks, long journeys, and the complex mix of curiosity and power that defines ocean voyages.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1798, Napoleon Bonaparte and his expeditionary force set sail from France to invade Egypt, bringing not only soldiers but also scholars and scientists. The campaign left a controversial military legacy but also sparked a wave of scientific and archaeological discovery, including the famous Rosetta Stone.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A grand, unsettling expedition that uprooted armies and scholars across cultures echoes the episode’s theme of travel as perspective-shift—though this time through a critical lens on conquest and the ‘moving meditation’ of world-changing journeys.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1942, in the aftermath of the Battle of the Coral Sea, the U.S. Navy’s Task Force 16 headed back to Pearl Harbor for crucial repairs. In a war defined by constant motion, this retreat to port was a rare moment of enforced pause, patching steel and nerves before the next Pacific clash.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A battered task force sailing home after a brutal battle fits the episode’s mood of weathering storms, seeking repair, and holding on to the sense that ‘it can’t rain forever’ even in wartime seas.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1776, during the American Revolutionary War, a Continental Army garrison surrendered at the Battle of The Cedars near Montreal. The skirmish showed how fragile early American campaigns in Canada were, with miscommunications and small detachments tipping the balance of an entire front.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: This small but symbolic Revolutionary War defeat sits in the broader story of rebellion against imperial power, echoing the episode’s focus on political resistance, struggle, and the soundtrack of dissent.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1900, Great Britain annexed Tonga Island in New Zealand’s Abel Tasman region, folding a tiny stretch of coastline into imperial administration. Today the island is better known for its golden beaches and marine life than its imperial paperwork, a quiet chapter of empire now reclaimed by tides and tourists.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: An island shifting from imperial claim to coastal sanctuary invites us to go below the surface of old maps and into the water itself, aligning with an episode about immersion, sea-like flow, and letting places be felt rather than owned.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1780, the New England Dark Day plunged parts of the northeastern United States and Canada into eerie, daytime darkness as smoke, fog, and weather blotted out the sun. Birds fell silent, people lit candles at noon, and many interpreted the phenomenon as a supernatural omen before science later pointed to massive forest fires as the likely cause.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A midday turned to night is like an unexpected ‘evening flow’ imposed by the sky itself, inviting people to drift beyond the expected day–night rhythm—just as this format invites listeners into unstructured, twilight moods.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1933, Finnish cavalry officer C. G. E. Mannerheim was promoted to field marshal, the highest rank in the Finnish army. His career would span tsarist Russia, independence, and war with the Soviet Union, making him a central figure in Finland’s 20th‑century survival narrative.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A field marshal leading armies and women leading bands both inhabit command roles in traditionally male-dominated arenas, connecting military hierarchy to the rebellious, amplified leadership of rock.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1802, Napoleon Bonaparte founded the Legion of Honour, a new order of merit meant to reward military and civil service regardless of noble birth. The red ribbon quickly became a symbol of both discipline and prestige, reshaping how modern states signal honor.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Where the Legion of Honour canonized excellence in service, this debut Sedna episode canonizes a personal ‘order of merit’ in music—honoring influences and friends that define their creative universe.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1743, French scientist Jean-Pierre Christin proposed a centigrade temperature scale in which 0° marked the freezing point of water and 100° its boiling point. His calibration helped standardize temperature measurement, paving the way for the Celsius scale that quietly underpins weather reports and scientific experiments worldwide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Christin’s centigrade scale standardized how we measure heat, much like Auto‑Tune standardized pitch correction; both are invisible tools that quietly reshape how we quantify and ‘correct’ continuous phenomena.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1922, the Young Pioneer Organization of the Soviet Union was founded as a mass youth movement, immersing millions of children in socialist rituals, camps, and songs. For many, it was both a playground and an early introduction to political life, with red neckerchiefs tying childhood to ideology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #26 - Canserbero
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the story of Canserbero, the Venezuelan rapper who reshaped Latin American hip hop with his raw voice and deeply reflective lyrics. A journey into his music, his vision, and the legacy of an artist who transformed personal and social struggles into powerful storytelling.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como una película y su tragedia, comedia y ficción

queue.match_reason: A state-run youth movement shaping young minds and a conscious rapper critiquing social systems both wrestle with ideology and identity, making this a pairing about how narratives imprint on the next generation.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1911, Parks Canada was created as the Dominion Parks Branch, becoming the world’s first national park service. From the Rocky Mountains to coastal reserves, it pioneered the idea that governments should actively protect landscapes not only for resources, but for wildlife, ecosystems, and future visitors.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

queue.match_reason: A national park service built to protect cherished landscapes resonates with an episode rooted in love for a specific island, both weaving policy and memory around the places that shape us.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 2016, EgyptAir Flight 804 disappeared from radar and crashed into the Mediterranean Sea en route from Paris to Cairo, killing all 66 people on board. The tragedy triggered multinational search efforts and intense speculation, illustrating how modern air travel still carries uncertainties even amid advanced technology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A fatal flight over the Mediterranean invites somber reflection on fragility and sudden loss, aligning with an episode about looking back on unforeseen events and slowly rebuilding after rupture.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 2007, a mass shooting began in Moscow, Idaho, ultimately leaving three victims dead and three injured before the perpetrator took his own life. The incident shook a small college town, raising difficult questions about violence, mental health, and the thin line between everyday life and catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #55 - JOMO
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores one of her own personality traits through the lens of psychology and music: the Joy of Missing Out. 

From the Need for Cognition to Deliberate Ignorance and Inattentional Blindness, the episode traces what it means to be someone whose brain demands depth before it gives energy, and what happens when the world around you expects the opposite.

Listen at your own pace, and let us know if you recognize yourself in this one.

Tracklist:
Burna Boy - Ye
2Pac - I Ain't Mad at Cha
Jet Life - Paper Habits
Travis Scott - Stop Trying to Be God
Andrea Laszlo De Simone - Il Regno Animale

queue.match_reason: A small town’s sudden trauma highlights how crowded, overstimulated worlds can mask deep distress, connecting to an episode that questions overstimulation and advocates for stepping back, reflecting, and choosing what we let in.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1961’s distant cousin in space history, the International Space Station itself traces its lineage through missions like STS-101, but its existence also depends on countless unsung resupply and maintenance flights. Each mission quietly knits together a patchwork of modules, experiments, and human stories into a single, orbiting habitat.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: The ISS is a ‘destination’ assembled over time by many journeys, paralleling this episode’s idea of travelling through musical territories and slowly building a shared, orbiting home of sound.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, 1962’s birthday gala sat at an intersection of politics and spectacle, but backstage was a tangle of rehearsals, last‑minute changes, and nervous performers. The iconic moment we remember was shaped by the invisible labor of musicians, staff, and technicians turning a cavernous arena into a single, breath‑held silence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A carefully staged arena moment that turned into cultural mythology mirrors the way dancefloors and performances become more than nights out—they become symbols of who gets to take up space in public spectacle.

queue.fact_text: On May 19, the Arctic and subarctic have seen both Franklin’s doomed ships and modern eco‑tourism voyages glide through the same cold waters, showing how narratives of conquest are slowly being replaced by stories of respect and observation. The same fjords that swallowed wooden hulls now echo with the hum of research vessels and camera shutters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: This fact revisits the transformation of Arctic waters from arenas of imperial ambition to spaces of quiet observation, directly echoing the episode’s focus on modern, respectful encounters with whales and wild seas.

queue.fact_text: Across the 19th and 20th of May in different years, explorers left ports, armies shifted fronts, youth movements were born, and forests dimmed daylight—reminding us how one calendar day can hold wildly different stories. May 19 is less a single anniversary than a mosaic, where science, war, nature, pop culture, and tragedy all leave their fingerprints on the same square of time.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A day stitched together from many timelines suits an episode that starts in one place and crescendos across continents—both are mosaics, where disparate textures blend into a single, hypnotic flow.

published.fact_text: On May 19, 1961, the Soviet probe Venera 1 silently became the first human-made object to fly past another planet, gliding by Venus after all contact with Earth had already been lost. Humanity’s first interplanetary flyby thus unfolded in complete radio silence, a milestone marked more by imagination than by data.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A silent spacecraft gliding past Venus turns cosmic emptiness into possibility, mirroring the episode’s theme of seeing ‘emptiness’ as creative space and shifting perspective toward new orbits.

published.fact_text: On May 19, 2000, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on STS-101 to deliver supplies and upgrades to the then-young International Space Station. The mission was a spaceborne housewarming, ferrying tools, oxygen, and hardware to turn a bare orbital outpost into a more livable home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

published.match_reason: A shuttle crew hauling supplies to make the ISS habitable is essentially turning a metal shell into a home in orbit, echoing the episode’s warmth about shared roofs and the feeling of ‘friend’s home’.

published.fact_text: On May 19, 1996, Space Shuttle Endeavour lifted off on STS-77, a mission packed with experimental payloads including inflatable structures and free-flying platforms. It was a laboratory in the sky, testing futuristic technologies in the frictionless classroom of orbit.
